algorithms.title: GCD, LCM & Sieve of Eratosthenes
algorithms.description: Core number theory primitives: GCD (Greatest Common Divisor) via Euclidean algorithm O(log min(a,b)), LCM = a*b/GCD, and the Sieve of Eratosthenes for finding all primes up to N in O(N log log N). These appear in problems involving fractions, modular arithmetic, counting divisors, and prime factorization.
algorithms.intuition: GCD: Euclidean insight — gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a%b). The remainder shrinks geometrically. Base case: gcd(a,0) = a. LCM: a and b share gcd as common factor, so LCM = a*(b/gcd(a,b)) to avoid overflow. Sieve: mark all multiples of each prime starting from p². If p is prime, p² is the first composite multiple not already marked by a smaller prime.
algorithms.steps: [{"step":1,"description":"GCD: while b != 0: temp=b, b=a%b, a=temp. Return a."},{"step":2,"description":"LCM: return a / gcd(a,b) * b (divide first to avoid overflow)."},{"step":3,"description":"Sieve: boolean[] composite = new boolean[N+1]. For i=2 to sqrt(N): if not composite[i], mark i*i, i*i+i, ... as composite."},{"step":4,"description":"Remaining unmarked positions are primes."}]
algorithms.timeComplexity: GCD: O(log min(a,b)) | Sieve: O(N log log N)

useCases.title: Fraction simplification
useCases.desc: Reduce a/b to lowest terms using GCD

useCases.title: Finding primes up to N
useCases.desc: Sieve gives all primes; used in prime factorization, RSA

useCases.title: Modular arithmetic
useCases.desc: GCD appears in modular inverse: gcd(a,MOD)=1 is required for inverse to exist

variants.title: Extended Euclidean Algorithm
variants.desc: Finds x,y such that ax+by=gcd(a,b) — used for modular inverse

variants.title: Segmented Sieve
variants.desc: Find primes in range [L,R] without storing entire sieve up to R

variants.title: Euler's Totient Function
variants.desc: φ(n) = count of integers coprime to n; computed with sieve in O(N log log N)

algorithms.title: Modular Exponentiation
algorithms.description: Computes a^n mod m in O(log n) using repeated squaring. Essential in cryptography (RSA), competitive programming (large number problems), and combinatorics (nCr mod p). Naive exponentiation takes O(n) multiplications and overflows for large n.
algorithms.intuition: a^n = (a^(n/2))^2 if n is even, a * a^(n-1) if n is odd. This is binary exponentiation: process bits of n. If bit k is set, multiply the current power of a into the result. Square a at each step. Total multiplications = O(log n).
algorithms.steps: [{"step":1,"description":"Initialize result = 1, base = a % m."},{"step":2,"description":"While n > 0:"},{"step":3,"description":"If n is odd (n & 1 == 1): result = result * base % m."},{"step":4,"description":"Square base: base = base * base % m."},{"step":5,"description":"Halve exponent: n >>= 1."}]

useCases.title: RSA encryption/decryption
useCases.desc: Compute m^e mod n and c^d mod n for large exponents

useCases.title: Fermat's little theorem
useCases.desc: a^(p-1) ≡ 1 mod p, used to compute modular inverse

useCases.title: Combinatorics with MOD
useCases.desc: Compute C(n,k) mod p = n! / (k! * (n-k)!) mod p using modular inverse

variants.title: Matrix exponentiation
variants.desc: Compute M^n for a matrix M in O(K³ log n) — Fibonacci in O(log n)

variants.title: Modular inverse
variants.desc: a^(-1) mod p = a^(p-2) mod p (when p is prime, by Fermat's little theorem)
